does: This paper proposes AcquisitionSynthesis, a method that leverages acquisition functions from active learning as reward signals to guide language models in generating higher-quality synthetic training data. Rather than relying on rejection sampling or larger models to identify valuable samples, the approach trains a language model to directly optimize for data informativeness and influence as measured by acquisition functions. The method is evaluated on verifiable domains including mathematics, medical question-answering, and coding tasks, demonstrating that acquisition-function-guided synthetic data improves downstream learner performance compared to baseline generation approaches.
connects: This work bridges active learning and synthetic data generation, two previously separate research directions. Active learning has long used acquisition functions to measure data value for model improvement, while recent work in synthetic data generation has explored rejection sampling and leveraging larger models as teachers. The paper extends prior curriculum learning and data synthesis approaches by introducing a quantitative, model-centric measurement framework absent in earlier generative methods. It builds on the intuition that knowing what makes data valuable for learning—captured by acquisition functions—should directly inform generation strategies, combining insights from both the active learning and synthetic data communities.
enables: This work opens avenues for more efficient synthetic data generation by making the value of generated samples measurable and optimizable. Future research could extend acquisition function-based synthesis to harder domains beyond verifiable tasks, explore domain-adaptive acquisition functions that account for specific downstream learner architectures, or combine multiple acquisition strategies in ensemble approaches. The method suggests a general principle for aligning synthetic data generation with model learning objectives, potentially enabling more sample-efficient training pipelines and reducing reliance on large proprietary models for data curation. Applications could include automated curriculum design and targeted data augmentation for low-resource settings.
